header: Head of B2B Unit / Quinto Andar

lesson.menu_desc: Formal vs informal email: polite requests & register -- 27 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 16 -- Email Mastery -- Tone & Register
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eduardo Chiba | Aula 16 -- Email Mastery

listenings.text: Hi Eduardo, it is Sarah. I am leaving you a quick voice note instead of typing, but please turn this into a proper email to the new partner. Keep it formal and concise. Start with a courteous greeting, something like Dear Mr. Costa. Then say that we were wondering if his team could send the signed contract by Friday. Add that we would appreciate it if he could confirm the kickoff date as well. Mention that the proposal is in the attachment, and ask him to let us know if anything is unclear. Close with a polite sign-off, Best regards, and your name and title. Please keep the recipient in mind. He is senior, so no slang and no one-line replies. Thanks.

Let me think about tone before I hit send. The same message can sound very different depending on the register. To a close colleague I might write, can you send me the file. That is fine internally, short and direct. But to a senior partner I have never met, that line is too blunt. I should soften it. Could you send me the file, or even, I was wondering if you could send me the file. The meaning is the same, but the second one is courteous. A good email is concise, but never cold. I open with the right salutation, I make my request politely, I point to the attachment, and I close with a clear sign-off. Tone is not decoration. To the recipient, tone is the message.

extra_audio.text: First, you open with a courteous salutation that fits the recipient. Then you state your purpose in one concise line. After that, you make your request politely, softening it with could you or I was wondering if. Next, you point the reader to the attachment and any next step. Finally, you close with a polite sign-off and your name.
